title: On Real Zeros of Holomorphic Hecke Cusp Forms and Sieving Short Intervals
speaker: Kaisa MatomÃ¤ki
abstract: A. Ghosh and P. Sarnak have recently initiated the study of so-called real zeros of holomorphic Hecke cusp forms, that is zeros on certain geodesic segments on which the cusp form (or a multiple of it) takes real values. In the talk I'll first introduce the problem and outline their argument that many such zeros exist if many short intervals contain numbers whose all prime factors belong to a certain subset of primes. Then I'll speak about new results on this sieving problem which lead to improved lower bounds for the number of real zeros.

proof_stages.label: Real-zero problem for holomorphic Hecke cusp forms
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up holomorphic Hecke cusp forms of weight k on the full modular group, their zeros in the fundamental domain, the real arcs delta_1, delta_2, delta_3, known equidistribution and Ghosh-Sarnak bounds, and the speaker's target improvements.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from real zeros to large Hecke coefficients in short intervals
proof_stages.summary: Using the Fourier expansion and Ghosh-Sarnak single-term dominance at suitable heights, sign changes and a parity trick reduce the production of many real zeros to finding many short intervals containing l with |lambda_f(l)| bounded below.

proof_stages.label: Prime dichotomy from the Hecke relation
proof_stages.summary: The relation lambda_f(p^2)=lambda_f(p)^2-1 splits primes into sets P_1 and P_2 so that every prime gives either a large lambda_f(p) or lambda_f(p^2), and primes in almost all short intervals yield admissible l=pq or l=p^2, recovering the k^{1/4-1/80} type bound and the RH variant.

proof_stages.label: General sieve formulation using semigroups generated by prime sets
proof_stages.summary: To remove the prime-interval loss, the argument replaces primes by products of boundedly many primes from P_i, formulates semigroups N_i generated by P_i, and reduces the zero problem to proving that more than half of the relevant short intervals meet such a semigroup.

proof_stages.label: Case 1: a prime set with local density above one half
proof_stages.summary: When P contains more than half the primes in some interval, a subset A of products p_1p_2n is counted from below by subtracting the P-complement contribution from an all-primes contribution, with Perron's formula and Dirichlet-polynomial mean values giving a positive lower bound in short intervals.

proof_stages.label: Case 2: uniform near-half density and half-dimensional sieving
proof_stages.summary: When no such dense interval exists, P has approximately half the primes locally, and a half-plus-delta-dimensional sieve gives almost-all short-interval upper bounds while long-interval multiplicative estimates give lower bounds, proving that a proportion just below one half, say 49%, of intervals are nonempty.

proof_stages.label: Bootstrapping past one half via products and additive combinatorics
proof_stages.summary: The 49% supply of good short intervals is amplified by multiplying elements from two good intervals and studying the sumset of their indices; either the sumset is large enough immediately or small doubling and a quantitative Freiman 3k-3 theorem force enough regularity to obtain more than 50% nonempty intervals.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and exponent barriers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that almost all forms fall into the easier first case, that the one-line exponent is limited by bounds for the first negative eigenvalue, and that the k^{1/4} barrier comes from the possibility that lambda_f(p) is usually small and one must use squares.

examples.name: Eisenstein-series zeros on the third real arc
examples.note: The speaker mentions Eisenstein series as a contrasting case where all zeros on the third arc are understood, highlighting that the cusp-form problem on that arc remains inaccessible.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites the Eisenstein-series case as a known contrast, stating that all its zeros lie on the third real arc without deriving or analyzing it.

passes.reason: The Eisenstein-series zero set is a standard infinite-family case with maximal concentration on the third real arc, and it is cited as a known contrast rather than to refute a claim.
Eisenstein series form a standard infinite family, and the cited zero set is structurally extremal because all zeros lie on the single real arc δ3 rather than being spread through the domain.
All zeros of the Eisenstein-series family lie on the third real arc, giving a maximally concentrated real-boundary case, used here only as contrast to the cusp-form situation.
All zeros of the Eisenstein series lie on the boundary real arc δ3, giving maximal real-arc concentration, and it is cited as a known special case rather than a refutation.
Eisenstein series form a standard infinite family whose zero set is extremal here because all zeros lie on the third real arc, and it is cited as a known contrast rather than a refutation.
For the standard infinite family of holomorphic Eisenstein series, the cited configuration is extremal because all zeros lie on the third real arc, and it is used only as a contrasting known case.

For holomorphic Eisenstein series for the full modular group, the zero set is used as a contrasting known special case where all zeros lie on the third real arc, a maximally concentrated and highly regular real-zero configuration rather than a refutation.

The Eisenstein-series zero set is invoked as a known contrast, not to refute a claim, and having all zeros on the third real arc is a maximal/concentrated boundary case among modular-form zero configurations.
It is cited as a known contrast case where the zero concentration is maximal—all zeros lie on the third real arc—rather than as a disproof of a claim.
Used as a known contrasting special case, with the Eisenstein-series zero set maximally concentrated by having all zeros on the third real arc.
Cited as a known contrasting case in which Eisenstein series attain the maximal concentration of zeros on the third real arc, not as a refutation.

Holomorphic Eisenstein series form a systematic family and the cited fact that all their zeros lie on the third real arc is a maximal concentration of real zeros, used only as a contrasting known case rather than to refute a claim.
